title: European Heritage Hub announces two European Heritage Label sites to host upcoming Intergenerational Climate Conversations
content: The EU-funded European Heritage Hub has selected two European Heritage Label sites to host the first-ever Intergenerational Climate Conversations, an initiative co-coordinated by Hub partners ICLEI Europe and Europa Nostra, with support from the European Heritage Label Bureau and their Youth Advisory Board, and Youth.Heritage.Europe. alongside the

title: Call for Applications: Hub Peer-learning Visit to Rotterdam
content:
The EU-funded European Heritage Hub invites city representatives and heritage professionals to apply for its upcoming peer-learning visit in Rotterdam, The Netherlands, taking place from 17-19 June 2026. Organised in partnership with the City of Rotterdam, this programme offers a unique opportunity to share good practices and acquire skills for addressing environmental, digital, and social challenges related to cultural heritage in local contexts.

The visit follows up on the Hub’s Open Call for Local Good Practices, launched in November 2025, recognising exemplary local solutions in cultural heritage that contribute to the green, digital, and social transformation of our society. Rotterdam was among the 10 selected practices having presented its project “Holy Houses Festival”, celebrating the city’s religious heritages and creativity, which will be the main focus of the visit.

creator: Liv Martin

title: Join Youth.Heritage.Europe.’s 2nd Youth Policy Debate exploring heritage as a driver for empowering local communities
content:
As an affiliated partner of the EU-funded European Heritage Hub, Youth.Heritage.Europe. is organising the second in a series of three Youth Policy Debates, focusing on the theme of “Heritage & Local Communities”. On 16 April 2026 at 14:00 CET, the event will bring together heritage professionals, researchers, and youth representatives to discuss how cultural heritage can act as a catalyst for community empowerment, social innovation, and inclusive local development.

Cultural heritage plays a vital role in supporting regeneration and cohesion, particularly in regions experiencing economic or social challenges. However, its potential often remains underutilised due to limited resources, insufficient community engagement, and governance structures that do not fully include local voices. This debate aims to address these challenges by exploring how participatory approaches and locally rooted initiatives can unlock the transformative power of heritage.

title: Hub launches Open Call for In Varietate Concordia opinion articles on culture, heritage and European values
content:
The European Heritage Hub is proud to announce a new Open Call for opinion articles and columns through its In Varietate Concordia initiative. We are inviting Europe’s cultural and intellectual community to share insights, perspectives, and solutions for the complex challenges facing our democracies today.

At a time when artistic freedom is under threat, and the dual pressures of disinformation and political polarisation are rising, culture and heritage must be at the heart of our social and political strategies. The mission of the In Varietate Concordia Columns is to highlight new and diverse perspectives on the key priorities that should define our path forward.

Who should apply?

We strongly encourage participation from across all European countries and diverse fields of expertise. This call is aimed at:

title: Europa Nostra Heritage Hub in Kraków expands “Mapping the Non-Governmental Heritage Sector” project to Northern and Western Europe
content:
As part of the project Mapping the Non-Governmental Heritage Sector in North and Western Europe (2026–2027), the Europa Nostra Heritage Hub in Kraków, operated by the International Cultural Centre in Kraków, has launched a survey phase addressed to non-governmental organisations active in the field of cultural heritage in Northern and Western Europe. The study is carried out in the framework of the EU-funded European Heritage Hub.

The survey is being conducted in eight countries covered by the second stage of the mapping: Belgium, Denmark, Finland, Germany, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Norway and Sweden. Its purpose is to collect comparable data on the functioning of non-governmental heritage organisations, including information on their fields of activity, organisational structures, cooperation with other stakeholders, and the main challenges faced by the sector.

The data collected through the questionnaire constitute a key element of the entire project. The success of the mapping largely depends on the participation of organisations in the survey, as only direct responses fr

creator: Liv Martin

title: Hub selects 10 new Local Good Practices across Europe from Open Call
content:
The EU-funded European Heritage Hub has announced the selection of 10 initiatives from its recent Open Call for Local Good Practices, aimed at identifying exemplary local practices in the field of cultural heritage that contribute to the triple transformation (green, digital, and social) of our society.

Running from November 2025 to January 2026, the call received 45 high-quality applications from 21 cities/regions across Europe. The selection criteria evaluated the alignment of submitted practices with the overarching themes of the Hub project, giving particular attention to the triple transformation and the innovative character of the initiatives. Additionally, the selection ensured a balanced representation of themes and locations.

This initiative will inform future capacity-building activities of the Hub. In 2026, peer-learning visits, each open to 20 participants, will be held in two cities selected among

creator: Liv Martin

title: The European Union Youth Orchestra celebrates 50th anniversary of its founding with concerts in Strasbourg, supported by the Hub
content:
On 8 March, 1976 the European Parliament passed a unanimous resolution to found the European Community Youth Orchestra – today, the EUYO. The EUYO was born from a conviction that young people from diﬀerent countries making music together is a compass that points us towards a better way to live.

Its co-founders perceived that the European Union is a cultural as well as an economic community, and that the enrichment of that community can inspire the world if its level is always one of excellence. Fifty years on and based in Grafenegg, Austria, the EUYO is one of the world’s great orchestras of young people, working with the world’s greatest soloists and conductors, and performing in the most iconic settings across five continents — from the Musikverein to Carnegie Hall.

To mark the 50th anniversary of its founding, the EUYO heads to the EU Capital of Strasbourg for a Spring Residency themed around the canonical and the new: past, present, and future. Its flagship concert of world premiere

creator: Liv Martin

title: Hub partner Youth.Heritage.Europe. launches new identity and strengthens youth leadership in European heritage sector
content:
On 19 February 2026, Youth.Heritage.Europe., formerly ESACH, officially unveiled its new name, visual identity and enriched mission during an online launch event, marking a major milestone for youth engagement in Europe’s cultural heritage sector.

The transition reflects the progress of a network that began as a student-led initiative and has grown into a structured European association representing young heritage professionals and researchers across the continent. However, the true centre of gravity of the event was not only the new identity, but the strengthening of youth leadership through the NextGen Heritage Ambassadors, developed in the frame of the EU-funded European Heritage Hub.

The launch featured keynote contributions from Glenn Micallef, European Commissioner for Intergener

creator: Liv Martin

title: Inaugural “In Varietate Concordia” Talk reflected on Europe’s ‘unfinished symphony’ through music
content:
The first In Varietate Concordia Talk, “Europe, variations on a theme”, took place at the Calouste Gulbenkian Foundation in Lisbon and was livestreamed via the European Heritage Hub’s YouTube channel on 23 February 2026.

Bringing together leading voices from the Iberian cultural sphere, the event featured conductor, composer and musician Jordi Savall; baritone and founder of the Centre Européen de Musique Jorge Chaminé; fado singer and curator of the Ponta Delgada 2026 – Portuguese Capital of Culture project, Kátia Guerreiro. The discussion was moderated by Sneška Quaedvlieg-Mihailović, Secretary General of Europa Nostra and Project Leader of the European Heritage Hub.

Marking forty years after Portugal and Spain joined the European Union in 1986, and on the eve

creator: Liv Martin

title: Hub’s Heritage Dialogue in Marseille explored shared heritage, identity and challenges across the Mediterranean
content:
On 11 February 2026, the European Heritage Hub held its sixth Heritage Dialogue at the Citadelle de Marseille (Fort Saint-Nicolas), hosted by ACTA VISTA, BAO Formation and La Citadelle de Marseille. Under the title “Mediterranean Crossings: Exploring Shared Heritage, Identity and Challenges,” the event brought together representatives from the European institutions, international organisations, civil society leaders, heritage practitioners and local actors to reflect on the role of cultural heritage in strengthening cooperation across the Mediterranean region.
